schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, being about to enter upon his own praises, uses much previous correction — he who remembers sins God remembered not, and who therefore says he was unworthy of the very name of the Apostles, is clearly seen even by the most insensate to be averse to speaking of himself, and only forced to it; Paul wishing the Corinthians would bear with him a little in his 'folly' and entreating their forbearance — the reluctant self-defence (Paul, compelled by the false apostles' boasting to speak in his own commendation, treating such self-praise as a 'folly' he engages in only under necessity, asking the Corinthians' patience while he does what is so distasteful to him for their sake).

themes: would to God ye could bear with me a little in my folly
and indeed bear with me
the reluctant self-defence
self-praise treated as a folly

schools.summary: Gill: Paul's concern was not on his own account or with selfish views but 'a godly jealousy,' a zeal of God which he was inspired with by God, for the honour and glory of God, even Jesus Christ who is God over all, and for the good of the church; Paul declaring that he is jealous over them with a godly jealousy, having espoused (betrothed) them to one husband, to present them a chaste virgin to Christ — the betrothal of the church to Christ (Paul, as the friend who had betrothed the Corinthian church to Christ as her one Husband, watching over her with a holy jealousy, like a father guarding a daughter's purity, longing to present her to Christ unspotted and faithful, and so fearing any seduction from her single-hearted devotion, v.3).

themes: I am jealous over you with godly jealousy
I have espoused you to one husband
present you as a chaste virgin to Christ
the church betrothed to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: jealousy is always attended with fear, care, and solicitude, whether in things natural or spiritual; the apostle, as things were in this church, could not but express his fears; Barnes: Paul had compared the church to a virgin soon to be presented as a bride to the Redeemer, and the mention suggested the image of Eve; Paul fearing lest, as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtlety, the Corinthians' minds be corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ — the danger of being led astray by deceivers (Paul, drawing on the fall of Eve, deceived by the serpent's craft, fearing the false apostles' subtle teaching may likewise seduce the church from its single, pure devotion to Christ, corrupting the simplicity of faith and love into something adulterated).

themes: as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtilty
corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ
the danger of seduction by deceivers
the single, pure devotion to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: 'he that cometh' may mean some particular man who came from Judea to Corinth under the character of a true apostle, or any one of the false apostles who came of their own accord, never sent by Christ or any of his churches; Paul observing, with irony, that if the newcomer preached another Jesus, another spirit, or another gospel than they had received, they might well bear with him — the rebuke of misplaced tolerance (Paul exposing the absurdity of the Corinthians' patience with the false apostles, for these brought no new and better Christ or gospel, which alone might excuse such welcome, but only a corruption of the one already received, so their forbearance was wholly undeserved).

themes: if he that cometh preacheth another Jesus
another spirit... another gospel
ye might well bear with him
the rebuke of misplaced tolerance

schools.summary: Gill: this is very modestly expressed — Paul does not assert and in a haughty, confident way affirm, but only supposes, or thinks it might be admitted, that he was not inferior to or behind in gifts, grace, and usefulness; Matthew Henry: after the preface, the apostle proceeds; Paul supposing that he was not a whit behind the very chiefest apostles — the quiet assertion of his apostolic standing (Paul, against the false apostles who exalted themselves over him, claiming, modestly as a mere 'supposing,' to be in no way inferior to even the greatest of the true apostles in gifts, grace, and labour, so the deceivers' pretended superiority over him was groundless).

themes: I suppose I was not a whit behind
the very chiefest apostles
the quiet assertion of his apostolic standing
modestly claimed

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was not an unlearned man, an idiot in speech, unskilful in language (his writings testify the contrary); he did not in his public ministry use the enticing words of man's wisdom, but he was not deficient in knowledge; Paul granting that though he be rude (unpolished) in speech he is not so in knowledge, having been thoroughly made manifest among them in all things — the substance above the style (Paul conceding, perhaps as the deceivers charged, a lack of rhetorical polish, but insisting he is not lacking in the knowledge of divine things, his whole conduct and teaching openly displayed among the Corinthians, who themselves could vouch for his thorough understanding).

themes: though I be rude in speech, yet not in knowledge
throughly made manifest among you in all things
substance above style
his knowledge manifest

schools.summary: Gill: Paul abased himself by behaving among them in a humble manner, in much fear and trembling, without pride or affectation of authority, by using a popular style suited to the common people, and by labouring with his own hands at his trade; Paul asking, with irony, whether he committed an offence by abasing himself (that they might be exalted) in preaching the gospel to them free of charge — the turning of his self-denial against the slander (Paul, whose refusal of pay the false apostles twisted into a proof he was no true apostle, asking whether his humility, working to support himself so the gospel cost them nothing, could really be reckoned a fault, when in fact it was for their exaltation).

themes: have I committed an offence in abasing myself
that ye might be exalted
I have preached to you the gospel of God freely
self-denial turned against the slander

schools.summary: Gill: 'I robbed other churches' means the churches of Macedonia — not that what he had of them was by force and rapine or by spoiling their substance, but what he had of theirs was freely communicated to him; the allusion is to a soldier's custom (using a conquered people), put with hyperbole; Paul declaring, in a striking figure, that he 'robbed' other churches, taking wages of them to do the Corinthians service — the cost of his free ministry borne by others (Paul, so far from burdening the Corinthians, supported in his work among them by the gifts of poorer Macedonian churches, a sacrifice he puts strongly, 'robbed,' to show how careful he was that the Corinthians should bear no expense for the gospel).

themes: I robbed other churches
taking wages of them, to do you service
the cost of his free ministry borne by others
that the Corinthians bear no expense

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul was burdensome to no one — literally, he did not lie as a dead weight upon them; Paul declaring that, when present and in want, he was chargeable to no one, his lack supplied by the Macedonian brethren, and that he had kept and would keep himself from being burdensome to the Corinthians — the resolve to take nothing from them (Paul, even when in real need among the Corinthians, refusing to be a financial burden, his wants met by gifts from Macedonia, firmly purposing to maintain this independence so that none could accuse him of self-interest).

themes: I was chargeable to no man
the brethren which came from Macedonia supplied
kept myself from being burdensome unto you
the resolve to burden no one

schools.summary: Gill: Paul joins an oath to show the firmness of his resolution — 'as the truth of Christ is in me' meaning as sure as Christ is truth who is in him, or as sure as the truth of grace and of the gospel is in his heart and mouth, so surely will he constantly persevere; Paul affirming, as with an oath by the truth of Christ in him, that no man shall stop him of this boasting (of preaching freely) in all the regions of Achaia — the unbreakable resolve of free ministry (Paul, with the solemnity of an oath, declaring his fixed determination that no one shall ever rob him of the ground of glorying he had in offering the gospel without charge throughout the whole region, so dear to him was this mark of disinterested love).

themes: as the truth of Christ is in me
no man shall stop me of this boasting
in the regions of Achaia
the unbreakable resolve of free ministry

schools.summary: Gill: some might insinuate that Paul's refusal to take support from the Corinthians (and his resolve to take nothing in Achaia) was because he did not love them, that he had no true affection for them; Paul answers by appealing to God, the searcher of hearts; Paul asking why he refuses their support — is it because he does not love them? — and answering with an appeal to God's knowledge — the heart laid open before God (Paul, anticipating the slander that his refusal of pay sprang from a lack of love or aloofness, repelling it not by argument but by calling the heart-searching God to witness the true affection that in fact moved him, the very opposite of indifference).

themes: wherefore? because I love you not?
God knoweth
the heart laid open before God
repelling the slander of indifference

schools.summary: Gill: as Paul preached the gospel freely at Corinth and in Achaia, so he was determined to do it for the future, for this reason chiefly; Barnes: the course he had been pursuing he would continue, preaching without demanding support; Paul declaring that he will keep doing as he does, to cut off occasion from those who seek occasion, that in their boasting they might be found even as he — the strategy against the deceivers (Paul keeping up his free preaching deliberately to rob the false apostles of any pretext to claim equality with him or to charge him with greed, so that if they would boast of working without pay they would be forced to match his own disinterested practice, which exposed their self-interest).

themes: what I do, that I will do
cut off occasion from them which desire occasion
wherein they glory, they may be found even as we
the strategy against the deceivers

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul calls them 'false apostles' precisely because they make pretence of preaching Christ, taking no money, and bringing no different gospel — for they make these pretences for the purpose of deceiving; 'deceitful workers,' for they do work indeed but pull up what has been planted (being well aware that otherwise they could not deceive); Paul plainly declaring that such men are false apostles, deceitful workers, transforming themselves into apostles of Christ — the exposure of the imposters (Paul tearing off the mask, naming the rivals not servants of Christ but counterfeit apostles and crafty deceivers who disguise themselves as true apostles, their fair appearance a deliberate fraud designed to ruin the church under the guise of building it).

themes: such are false apostles, deceitful workers
transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ
the unmasking of the false apostles
their fair appearance a deliberate fraud

schools.summary: Gill: this need not be wondered at, nor is it any new or strange thing, nor should it be thought incredible that there are such persons; Barnes: since Satan himself is capable of appearing to be an angel of light, it is not to be deemed strange that those in his service should do likewise; Paul declaring that it is no marvel the false apostles disguise themselves, for Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light — the master-pattern of deception (the imposture of the false apostles no surprise, since their master Satan is the great disguiser, who clothes himself in the appearance of light, truth, and holiness to deceive, so his agents' counterfeit holiness is but the imitation of his own, Satan deceiving not by open evil but by the appearance of good).

sides.position: Not by open evil but by disguising himself as 'an angel of light' — clothing himself in the appearance of light, truth, and holiness; his agents' counterfeit holiness imitates his own disguise

themes: and no marvel
Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light
the master-pattern of deception
Satan the great disguiser

schools.summary: Gill: it is no strange and wonderful thing, easily believed, no man need hesitate to believe it, that Satan's ministers be transformed into ministers of righteousness, since the devil himself, an angel of darkness, is transformed into an angel of light; Paul declaring that it is no great thing if Satan's ministers be transformed as ministers of righteousness, whose end shall be according to their works — the disguise of evil agents and their certain doom (since Satan disguises himself, no surprise that his servants, the false apostles, masquerade as ministers of righteousness, putting on a show of holiness; yet the solemn warning that, whatever their disguise, their final end shall answer to their true, evil works, not their fair pretence).

themes: his ministers also be transformed as the ministers of righteousness
whose end shall be according to their works
the disguise of evil agents
their end answering to their works, not their pretence

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was obliged to praise himself, in vindication of his own character against the false apostles, for the sake of the gospel he preached, and for the advantage and welfare of the Corinthians, that they might not be imposed upon and carried away by the insinuations of these deceitful men; Paul saying again, 'let no man think me a fool; if otherwise, yet as a fool receive me, that I may boast a little' — the repeated disclaimer before boasting (Paul, returning to the distasteful task of self-commendation, again pleading that none reckon him truly foolish for it, but, if they will so judge, to bear with him as one playing the fool, that he may glory a little, as the necessity of defending the gospel required).

themes: let no man think me a fool
if otherwise, yet as a fool receive me
that I may boast myself a little
the repeated disclaimer before boasting

schools.summary: Gill: Paul speaks this in vindication and commendation of himself on the subject of glorying; Barnes: the phrase 'not after the Lord' may mean either that Paul does not speak this by inspiration or claiming to be inspired, or, more probably, that he does not speak in the Lord's usual manner (cf. 1 Corinthians 7:12); Paul qualifying that what he speaks in this boasting he speaks not after the Lord (not by his ordinary direction) but as it were foolishly, in this confidence of boasting — the careful distinction in his self-defence (Paul, even while compelled to boast, marking that this self-commendation is not in the manner of Christ's lowliness, who sought not his own glory, but a thing forced on him, spoken as if foolishly, lest any think boasting itself a Christian virtue).

themes: I speak it not after the Lord
but as it were foolishly
in this confidence of boasting
the boasting forced, not Christ's manner

schools.summary: Gill: 'many glory after the flesh' means with respect to external things — high birth and parentage, carnal descent, circumcision, learned education, and the like — of which the false apostles, being Jews, boasted, who were many; though a multitude is not to be followed to do evil, Paul thought that since so many were indulged by this church in such boasting, he might do so too; Paul declaring that, since many glory after the flesh, he will glory also — the condescension to the boasters' level (Paul, seeing the false apostles boast of fleshly external advantages, willing for once to meet them on their own ground and boast likewise, not because such glorying is good but to show that even by their own carnal measure he is not their inferior).

themes: seeing that many glory after the flesh
I will glory also
meeting the boasters on their own ground
not their inferior by their own measure

schools.summary: Gill: the Corinthians bore with the false apostles, who were fools — continually proclaiming their folly, boasting of themselves, ascribing to themselves what did not belong to them, puffed up by their fleshly minds; they indulged these men in their folly, and that with pleasure and delight, not only winking at it but being pleased with it; Paul observing, with irony, that the Corinthians suffer fools gladly since they are themselves wise — the sharp irony of the rebuke (Paul, with cutting irony, saying the 'wise' Corinthians put up gladly with the foolish boasting of the false apostles, the very fact that such wise people tolerate fools being a backhanded reproach of their misplaced patience).

themes: ye suffer fools gladly
seeing ye yourselves are wise
the cutting irony of the rebuke
the wise tolerating fools

schools.summary: Gill: the Corinthians not only tolerated the foolish boasting of these men with pleasure, but patiently and stupidly bore their oppressions, injuries, and insults — things intolerable, which no man of any sense and wisdom would ever suffer — yet they took all quietly, made no objection, but patiently submitted; Paul listing the indignities the Corinthians endured from the false apostles — being brought into bondage, devoured, taken from, lorded over, and even smitten on the face — the abject submission rebuked (Paul exposing how the Corinthians meekly endured from the deceivers the grossest tyranny: enslaving their consciences, devouring their substance, exploiting and despising and insulting them, a degrading servility all the more shameful set beside their coldness toward him).

themes: if a man bring you into bondage
if a man devour you... take of you... exalt himself
if a man smite you on the face
the abject submission to tyranny rebuked

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul again draws back and uses depreciation and correctives beforehand, although he has already said many such things ('would that ye could bear with me in a little foolishness'; 'let no man think me foolish; if ye do, yet as foolish receive me'; 'that which I speak, I speak not after the Lord but as in foolishness'), softening the appearance of self-praise; Paul saying, with irony, that he speaks as if to his reproach, as though he had been weak (in not tyrannizing them as the false apostles did), yet wherein any is bold he too is bold — the ironic turning of the charge (Paul 'confessing' he was too 'weak' to oppress and fleece them as the deceivers did, but declaring that in whatever real ground of boasting any dares to glory, he, speaking foolishly, will dare as boldly).

themes: I speak as concerning reproach, as though we had been weak
whereinsoever any is bold, I am bold also
the ironic turning of the charge
too 'weak' to oppress them

schools.summary: Gill: the nation of the Jews were called Hebrews (not from Abraham, which the Hebrew language will not admit, but otherwise); Paul matches each claim — Hebrew, Israelite, seed of Abraham — for the false apostles boasted of these national and covenant privileges; Paul answering the false apostles' boast of Jewish descent by claiming each title for himself — the equality in covenant privilege (Paul, meeting the deceivers' pride in their Jewish pedigree, showing he lacks nothing they could claim: a Hebrew of pure descent and language, a true Israelite of the covenant people, and a son of Abraham, the three titles covering descent, covenant, and promise, so their boasted privileges gave them no advantage over him).

themes: are they Hebrews? so am I
are they Israelites? so am I
are they the seed of Abraham? so am I
equality in covenant privilege

schools.summary: Gill: Paul could have answered that they were not ministers of Christ (being neither sent by Christ nor preachers of him, but false apostles and deceitful workers seeking their own), but chose not to litigate the point, allowing it by a rhetorical concession, and replies that he is more; Paul declaring that, though they claim to be ministers of Christ, he is more — in more abundant labours, stripes above measure, frequent imprisonments, and deaths often — the proof of ministry by suffering (Paul, granting foolishly the deceivers' claim, asserting that he far surpasses them, and proving it not by eloquence or pedigree but by the toils and torments he has borne for Christ, beginning the great catalogue of apostolic sufferings).

themes: are they ministers of Christ? I am more
in labours more abundant, in stripes above measure
in prisons more frequent, in deaths oft
the proof of ministry by suffering

schools.summary: Gill: there is no account in Acts or elsewhere of any of these five scourgings, but no doubt of them; the number agrees with Jewish custom — the original law for scourging (Deuteronomy 25:3) allowed forty stripes, but the Jews gave thirty-nine ('forty save one') to avoid exceeding the law by a miscount; Paul recounting that five times he received from the Jews forty stripes save one — the repeated synagogue scourgings (Paul, five separate times, suffering the severe Jewish penalty of thirty-nine lashes, the legal limit short of forty, enduring this cruel and shameful punishment at the hands of his own countrymen for the sake of the gospel, none of which the book of Acts even records).

themes: five times received I forty stripes save one
of the Jews
the severe Jewish penalty
thirty-nine lashes borne for the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the beating with rods (wands) was a Roman punishment, distinct from the Jewish scourging with cords; Acts records but one such beating (at Philippi) and one stoning (at Lystra, Acts 14:19), though Paul suffered more; Paul recounting that he was thrice beaten with rods, once stoned, thrice shipwrecked, and a night and a day in the deep — the manifold perils endured (Paul suffering the Roman rod three times, stoned and left for dead once, shipwrecked three times besides the later one of Acts 27, and once spending a night and day adrift in the open sea, a list of dangers that far outran anything the boastful false apostles had borne).

themes: thrice was I beaten with rods, once was I stoned
thrice I suffered shipwreck
a night and a day I have been in the deep
manifold perils far beyond the false apostles'

schools.summary: Gill: Paul journeyed often through several countries and kingdoms to preach the gospel, from Jerusalem round about to Illyricum; Barnes: in perils of waters (rivers, floods, crossing streams), of robbers (many regions, especially Arabia, infested), and the rest; Paul recounting his constant journeyings and the perils that beset him on every side — from rivers, robbers, his own countrymen (Jews), the heathen (Gentiles), in city and wilderness and sea, and among false brethren — the danger from every quarter (Paul's life of travel for the gospel hemmed about with peril at every turn, from nature and from men, from Jew and Gentile, in every place, the most painful being the last, the danger from false brethren who betrayed him).

themes: in journeyings often, in perils of waters
perils of robbers... countrymen... heathen
perils in the city... wilderness... sea
in perils among false brethren

schools.summary: Gill: these were through long journeys and frequent preaching — 'in labour and trouble,' troublesome labour, for all labour is not so (some, as hunting, is delightful), but the apostle's labours were painful and troublesome to the flesh, though he had much inward spiritual delight in them; Barnes: the weariness resulting from travel and toil; Paul recounting his weariness and painfulness, frequent sleepless nights (watchings), hunger and thirst, frequent fastings, cold and nakedness — the daily privations of the apostle (beyond the dramatic dangers, Paul bearing a constant round of bodily hardship: exhausting toil, lost sleep, want of food and drink, and exposure to cold without sufficient clothing, the hidden, grinding cost of a life poured out for the gospel).

themes: in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often
in hunger and thirst, in fastings often
in cold and nakedness
the daily privations of the apostle

schools.summary: Gill: besides those things that are without (the outward things already mentioned, and many others too tedious to relate), there came upon Paul daily the care of all the churches — not only of all the churches but of all believers; Barnes notes this in addition to the external trials; Paul adding that, besides all the outward sufferings, there pressed on him daily the care of all the churches — the crowning, inward burden (beyond the bodily perils and privations, Paul carrying a ceaseless weight of pastoral anxiety, the daily concern for the welfare, purity, and steadfastness of every church he had founded, an inward burden heavier than any outward trial).

themes: beside those things that are without
that which cometh upon me daily
the care of all the churches
the crowning inward burden of pastoral care

schools.summary: Gill: whatever church or believer was weak in the faith, Paul was concerned for them, to instruct, establish, and strengthen them, suiting his discourses, reasonings, and language to their capacity, that they might understand him and take in right notions of things; Paul asking who is weak without his sharing the weakness, and who is offended (made to stumble) without his burning — the sympathy of the pastor's heart (Paul's care for the churches no cold duty but a living fellow-feeling, so that he felt the weakness of every weak believer as his own and was inflamed with grief and indignation whenever any was made to stumble, his heart bound up with the spiritual state of all).

themes: who is weak, and I am not weak?
who is offended, and I burn not?
the sympathy of the pastor's heart
feeling each one's weakness

schools.summary: Gill: glorying was not agreeable to Paul; he was not fond of it, it was a subject he did not delight to dwell upon; what he had done was by force and through necessity, compelled by the boasts of the false apostles, and since he must needs glory to stop their mouths, he would glory in his infirmities; Paul declaring that, if he must glory, he will glory of the things that concern his infirmities — the paradoxical boast in weakness (Paul, forced against his will to boast, choosing for his subject not his strengths or successes but his weaknesses and sufferings, the very things the world counts shameful, so turning the whole exercise of glorying upside down, finding his true ground of boasting in what proved his dependence on God).

themes: if I must needs glory
I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities
the paradoxical boast in weakness
glorying in what proved his dependence on God

schools.summary: Gill: these words are in the form of an oath, a solemn appeal to God, who knows all things, for the truth of the whole he had declared in the foregoing verses and of the remarkable deliverance he is about to relate — God, the searcher of hearts, the omniscient being to whom all things are open and manifest; Paul calling God, the blessed Father of the Lord Jesus, to witness that he does not lie — the appeal to the omniscient God (Paul, lest any think his catalogue of sufferings exaggerated, sealing it with a solemn oath, invoking the eternally blessed God who knows all things as witness that every word is true, so grave a thing it was to be doubted in his integrity).

themes: the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
which is blessed for evermore
knoweth that I lie not
the appeal to the omniscient God

schools.summary: Gill: Aretas (Al-Hareth) was a king of Arabia, of the family of the Gassanii, among whom were many of this name, who for some hundreds of years ruled over Syria, of which Damascus was the metropolis; his governor kept the city, seeking to seize Paul; Paul recounting that at Damascus the governor under King Aretas garrisoned the city, desiring to apprehend him — the peril at the very outset of his ministry (Paul, choosing to close his catalogue with one signal instance, recalling how, early in his Christian life, Acts 9:23-25, the governor under Aretas set a watch on Damascus to seize him, a danger so grave it forced the famous escape, v.33).

themes: the governor under Aretas the king
kept the city... with a garrison
desirous to apprehend me
the peril at the outset of his ministry

schools.summary: Gill: the house in which he was, like Rahab's, was built upon the wall of the city, and as she let down the spies by a cord through the window, and as David was let down by Michal, so the apostle was let down by the brethren in a basket through a window, as Jeremiah was let down (the Septuagint using the same word); Paul recounting that he was let down through a window in a basket by the wall and so escaped the governor's hands — the lowly deliverance as his crowning 'boast' (Paul ending his catalogue of glorying not with a triumph but with this humbling picture of himself smuggled out of a city in a basket, the great apostle's most fitting boast being an escape that displayed not his might but his weakness and God's preserving care).

themes: through a window in a basket was I let down by the wall
and escaped his hands
the lowly deliverance as his crowning boast
displaying his weakness and God's preserving care
